The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango
(29) The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango - Connecting to Atlas MongoDB

We already signed up for MongoDB Atlas and played with MongoDB Compass. So the obvious next step is actually wiring it into our own project. In this post I'll show you how little code it takes to point your app at the remote database.
The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango (28): Browsing Your Atlas Database with MongoDB Compass

In the last chapter we set up a remote MongoDB database over on Atlas. But now what? How do we actually use the thing? This chapter walks through connecting to your remote database with MongoDB Compass, the official GUI tool — copying the connection string, swapping in your password, and poking around your collections.
Testing MongoDB Reads with Mocha: find, findOne, and Pagination

Now we move on to writing tests for reading data. We'll cover find, findOne, and the pagination concept using limit, skip, and sort. Along the way I'll show you a sneaky gotcha with comparing MongoDB ObjectIds, and how a single toString() call saves the day.
(16) The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango - The Weird Thing About Mongoose Update

Let's circle back to Update for a bit. There's actually something pretty weird about Update that I never covered, so I'm setting aside a whole post for it. If you've ever wondered why your Schema validation suddenly stops working when you update a document, this one's for you.
(12) The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango - Mongoose Create and Model

We've already met Schema, SchemaType, and Schema Validators. But so far all we've done is tell Mongoose what type our data is — we haven't actually used any of it yet. In this post we finally put it to work: we'll turn a Schema into a Model and save our first document into MongoDB.
(9) The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango - Mongoose: Connecting to MongoDB and Writing Your First Schema

Next up we're heading into the Express development chapters, which means we'll start using Mongoose to connect to the database. But the very first thing you need to get familiar with is the Schema. In this post I'll walk through connecting to MongoDB with Mongoose and defining your first user Schema.
(8) The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango - MongoDB - ODM

Before we start writing code, let's talk about what ODM and ORM actually are. We're going to use MongoDB's ODM, but it helps to understand the difference between these two first. In short, both act as a bridge between your code and the database, so you don't have to poke at the database directly.
Mango Diaries #7: MongoDB CRUD — Delete (the easy one)

This is the last chapter of MongoDB CRUD — Delete. And honestly, once you've gone through the Create chapter, Delete feels pretty easy. We'll cover deleteOne and deleteMany, plus one nasty gotcha that can wipe your entire collection in a single line.
(4) The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango - MongoDB CRUD: Read

We've already inserted plenty of data into MongoDB, but how do we actually know it made it in there? In this post we'll learn the query syntax — starting with the basic find(), then working through the most common conditional operators like $gt, $lt, $and, and $or.
The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango (1): Meeting MongoDB and Installing It

Time for another learning log, and this time it's all about MongoDB. Since "Mongo" sounds a lot like "mango" in Chinese, I named this series "The Fullstack Dev Forced to Eat Mango." In this first post I'll cover what MongoDB actually is, how it differs from SQL, what BSON is, and how to install and run it on both Mac and Windows.
